svg.title: Tracking pipeline overview
svg.desc: Overview diagram of the multi-animal tracking pipeline from video input through detection, assignment, lifecycle management, backward replay, and post-processing.
svg.text: Current Tracking Algorithm
Detection, motion prediction, association, reconciliation, and export in one operational view
1. Video Ingest
Frames are read from disk, optionally prefetched, resized, and
paired with ROI masks and image adjustments before detection.
2. Detection
Either background subtraction or YOLO OBB emits per-frame
measurements, size cues, confidence values, and detection IDs.
Cache Layer
Raw detections are written to `.npz` cache files so tracking can
be replayed, tuned, or run backward without re-inference.
3. Prediction
A vectorized Kalman manager predicts `[x, y, theta, vx, vy]` for
every active slot, rotates process noise by heading, and keeps
young tracks conservative until enough evidence accumulates.
4. Association
Detections are matched to predictions using weighted motion,
orientation, area, aspect, and optional pose-shape agreement.
Established tracks use Hungarian or greedy assignment.
Pose-Aware Features
If pose extraction is enabled, directed heading and normalized
keypoint prototypes refine orientation and can veto bad matches.
Low-visibility cases fall back to OBB axis orientation.
5. Track Lifecycle
Matched tracks are corrected, unmatched tracks age into
`occluded` or `lost`, and distant free detections can respawn
previously lost slots with fresh trajectory IDs.
6. Forward and Backward Replay
Forward runs create the main hypothesis. Optional backward runs
reuse cached detections in reverse order to produce a second
trajectory hypothesis for conservative consensus merging.
7. Post-Processing and Export
Trajectory cleaning, merge resolution, relinking, interpolation,
CSV export, and optional crop datasets turn raw tracks into
analysis-ready artifacts for downstream biology workflows.
Cached detections and pose properties
feed replay, tuning, and reproducibility.
